title: Killing the Messenger: David's Policy or Politics?
author: Pamela Tamarkin Reis
publisher: Journal for the Study of the Old Testament 31.2 (SAGE Publishing)
allowedUseNotes: Copyrighted, paywalled peer-reviewed journal article. Summary in project's own words, drawn from the publicly visible abstract and secondary discussions of the article's argument, not the full paywalled text.
summary: A close literary-critical reading of David's two messenger-killing episodes (the Amalekite in 2 Samuel 1, and Rechab and Baanah in 2 Samuel 4) arguing that executing the messenger is not a fixed 'policy' of David's but a political calculation made case by case: David kills messengers only where he stands to gain politically from disavowing the news, not simply because the news is bad. Directly engages this project's open discrepancy question: Reis reads David as genuinely deceived by the Amalekite's invented account in 2 Samuel 1 (the messenger lied about killing Saul), with David only learning the true circumstances of Saul's death later, by 2 Samuel 4. This is a named, checkable critical-scholarship treatment supporting the 'self-serving fabrication' reading already carried in claim-amalekite-messenger-account's scholarlyViews (view-amalekite-fabrication), rather than the alternative 'genuine find, embellished' reading.
confidenceNotes: TO VERIFY against primary copy: this project accessed the article's argument only through its publicly visible SAGE abstract and secondary web summaries paraphrasing it, not the paywalled full text. The specific reading attributed here (David is genuinely deceived, not merely judging a confession he suspects is false) is corroborated across more than one independent search-result summary of the same abstract, which is why it is treated as citable at this stage, but full-article page-level verification (exact pages within 167-191) has not been done.
